data.title: Aurubis - Up And Down, But The Eventual Upside Can Result In Good RoR
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

In this article, I will update my thesis on Aurubis (OTCPK:AIAGF) (OTCPK:AIAGY), a German company in recycling and metal basic materials that I've covered for quite some time. My position is slightly in the negative due to relatively problematic trends for the company. Not just because of a relatively comprehensive and large theft which the company was the victim of, but also due to macro and some other trends. These things are set to impact the company's prospects and returns for at the very least the 2025 fiscal, and possibly more

It's also not unlikely that there may be other headwinds that could further impact this company's near-term appeal. But as I've shown you in other companies when it comes to valuation investing, what matters is that you buy at the right price and have the patience to wait for this to materialize. My last article on Aurubis can be found here. As I said, not been an impressive RoR since then - but the short term does not dissuade me if the short-term returns are on the negative side.

In this article, I will review the latest update for Aurubis, and see where the company can and likely will (in my estimate) go in the next few years. To give you a bit of a sneak peek, we're talking about a company that, I believe, will outperform in the longer term but may take some time to get there.

You can find my latest article on this company here, and peruse it at your leisure - here is my latest update.

Aurubis - The upside is non-trivial, but will take time to materialize

The latest results we have to look at are the 9M/3Q results for the 23/24 period reported in August of this year. That means we have some time until the full-year results. The company managed a relatively stable to growing performance during the 9M period, offsetting some of the early negative indications that we saw during 1Q.

There was an operating EBT of €333M, which was up on a YoY basis to the €257M of last year, an almost stable ROCE on and operating basis, and a forecast that to the full year goes up to €480M due to a potentially very strong 4Q24.

The quarter came in with positive trends for the concentrate trends, significant improvements in the metal results, and higher overall earnings from the company's copper premium. Demand trends for wire rods were higher and there is continued implied growth here, as well as lower overall energy costs.

The negative trends here were decreased revenues for sulfuric acid, and lower incomes from refining charges as well as some higher cost trends in these segments.

Some operationally relevant facts for the quarter include also that Aurubis' largest maintenance shutdown of the Hamburg site was completed on time and successfully. The ROCE was also impressive when you consider the degree of deployed CapEx for growth projects. Because once the earnings and EBT for these growth projects start contributing positively, we're talking about some very interesting upside.

The company's net cash flow improved as well, and this was again, despite a very comprehensive and complete shutdown in the Hamburg asset.

Aurubis remains primarily a macro-exposed sort of company/business. It's dependent on a number of market conditions - such as the CFR Brazil spot for Sulfuric Acid, the FX of EUR/USD, refining charges in Europe, Copper premiums, and copper concentrate contract levels for TC/RC.

The current trends show the company's gross margins being roughly at previous-year levels, but gross profit is significantly improved. Metal results are a big reason for this, offsetting any weakness in Premiums and treatment charges.

And while costs are up, the mix of costs is now even more favorable, with fewer consumables, less energy, and fewer external services. Personnel costs and others are likely up a bit due to the many projects the company has ongoing here.

And despite the company's share price evolution since my last article, the company's financial position remains very solid. CapEx is up, and net cash flow is down slightly, but the company remains close to a 55% Equity ratio, with the target here being above 40%. Debt coverage, meaning net financial liabilities/RTM EBITDA, is at 0.5x, with a target of below 3x. No matter what has happened to the company as of late, the company remains undoubtedly conservative here.

A quick segment review. Multimetal recycling was decent. The company saw good volumes across all areas, and despite a lower EBIT and ROCE, the company is mostly a slave to the global trends here - not much the company can do when charges are the way they are.

Custom Smelting/Products is what really shone (among other things) here. The company nearly doubled the EBIT and EBT in this segment, with a 390 bps ROCE improvement despite an almost flat volume. This once again reflects a very clear correlation to the overall macro here, even before looking at the graph with the result split below.

These are the two segments where Aurubis is active. And the recent quarter in August also resulted in a confirmation of the upside here, with clarity on just how much the result will depend on the macro. The company's operating EBT is expected to land between €380M to €390M, with a ROCE where the upper and lower forecast end has a spread of 400 bps- and that with only one quarter left. This again shows us the volatility of the company.

A word instead on growth projects. The company has continued to invest in a modernized tank house in Lünen, and the US-based Aurubis Richmond facility has really come into its own as well.

And with the company pursuing growth options that I consider to be very interesting indeed, there isn't much reason to believe the company is going to decline or to see massively unfavorable trends in the near future. What sort of growth options, you might ask?

Well, what about battery recycling? The company is working on plans for an asset that works in this field, and given the amount of EVs some believe will be around, and even just the amount of EVs around today, there isn't much reason to believe that there isn't going to be a demand here. But this is just one of several projects, and it's one not even clarified all that much. The company's pipeline for the next few years is extremely well-filled, to the degree that we'll see project after project of expansion coming online, with some very interesting effects on earnings. I believe the market is actually underestimating these effects.

Also, the C-suite improvements after the scandal with theft and oversight should not be underestimated, either. A company under the "loupe" is less likely, in my experience, to repeat or to make major mistakes. And you can see that with the new management, the company has really moved almost everyone into a new position.

And with regard to the criminal story here, there have not been any new findings since April of this year. This means that the company works under the assumption that all the negative effects of the criminal behavior began in 2023, which means that no further restatement is required as of this point beyond the restating of 22/23.

This means that for the valuation, things are looking as follows.

Aurubis Valuation - There's an upside, and it's in the future, but it seems very likely to me

All the positive things I'm about to say need to be taken into consideration or in context with the company's market position - exposed to some very volatile overall trends. So the company may go up and down much more violently than expected - because if you take even a glance at the 20-year average, you'll note that this has been a clear part of the company's pattern (Source: FAST Graphs Paywalled Link). In fact, this company's trading and earnings pattern does warrant showing in this article, given how very volatile it is - it almost looks like the European Alps.

So, as you can see, Aurubis is probably not for you if you're easily scared by valuation or earnings ups and downs. But the positive is when the company goes down, it typically goes up again just as quickly.

As you can see, for the next few years, beyond 2024-2025, you're looking at a slight dip, followed by yet another climb. And if we take even close to the company's valuation average into account, then this company can be considered cheap.

Aurubis typically manages a 13-15x P/E. It currently trades at 7.8x. The forecast accuracy is essentially no more accurate than a coin flip, with a slight tendency towards hitting targets rather than not hitting them in the long term. If we assume 12x P/E, the very low end of the company's averages, to be valid, then this gives us a 2027E upside of 20% per year, or 71% total RoR in 3-4 years. If we go up to 13-14x P/E, the higher end, we have closer to 25%, which comes to 88-90% RoR.

Add to this that I truly do believe the company is being undervalued here in terms of its growth projects and expansions, and we have a company that, almost in every scenario except a fundamental decline, has the potential for outperformance.

It is these sorts of companies that I tend to look for when I invest. Aurubis yield is not impressive. It's currently only at 2.2% despite the low valuation. So capital appreciation and potential dividend growth is what we have to go on here - which based on historicals seems a little unclear. Aurubis does not tend to use dividends to reward shareholders beyond its typical one. Still, based on what's being demanded here for the company's assets and equity, I say this is undervalued - and I am not the only one.

Aurubis has 9 analysts following the company - they go from a €52/share low target to a €93/share high target, with a near-€70/share average. However, out of 9, only 3 believe the company is attractive here. I interpret this as being a product of the uncertainty in the near term. It does not bother me for the longer-term estimate, but I can understand that they are somewhat dubious.

For that reason, my updated thesis for Aurubis AG is as follows - but I do maintain my PT here, and I also may buy more of the company relatively soon to increase my position here.

I do believe this company has the potential to outperform, I do believe it to be cheap. Ergo, I rate it a "Buy" here.

Thesis

  • Aurubis is a market-leading German metallurgy company in the segment of copper, other non-ferrous metals, and precious metals, as well as the byproduct of Sulfuric Acid. The company has one of the most extensive expertise on the planet in this particular field, and it deserves more than the attention it's getting here at this time.
  • As of the latest quarterly report, some of the expectations for 2024E are somewhat more crystallized, and what I would consider clearer than before. This enables us to act with more confidence and also maintain our PT and positive stance for this company.
  • I say that Aurubis remains a "BUY" at around €80/share, as of October of 2024 or below for the common. I have looked at options plays available for this company, but for the time being, would not consider this to be relevant due to low premiums. I consider the common share to be better, and I'm adding to my position here.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

There are still enough positives here, and I would now also argue the company is getting closer and closer to "cheap".

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: NBSM: Can This Active SMID-Cap ETF Justify Its 25.52x Forward P/E?
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The Neuberger Berman Small-Mid Cap ETF (NYSEARCA:NBSM) is an actively managed small/mid-cap blend fund launched on March 30, 2024, with a 0.74% expense ratio after waivers. While the fee is high, NBSM's main problems include a sky-high 25.52x forward P/E ratio, poor diversification, and a composition only suitable for down markets. In my view, it's not a great small/mid-cap solution, so I've assigned it a "sell," and I look forward to explaining why below.

NBSM Overview

Strategy Discussion

According to its website, Neuberger Berman is a private, independent, employee-owned investment manager with offices in 26 countries managing a combined $509 billion in assets under management. Their ETF offerings are limited, and on the equity side, have relatively low assets under management:

  • Carbon Transition & Infrastructure ETF (NBCT): $33.51 million
  • China Equity ETF (NBCE): 6.37 million
  • Core Equity ETF (NBCR): $235.30 million
  • Disrupters ETF (NBDS): $20.24 million
  • Japan Equity ETF (NBJP): $13.75 million
  • Next Generation Connected Consumer ETF (NBCC): $6.56 million
  • Small-Mid Cap ETF (NBSM): $198.69 million

As shown, NBSM is the firm's second-largest active equity ETF, with $198.69 in assets under management. In addition, its gross expense ratio is 0.87% (0.74%) after waivers), and the firm offers the following reasons to invest:

1. The relative lack of analyst coverage in the SMID-cap universe can result in companies that are underappreciated and mis-priced versus their potential, creating opportunity for active managers to add value.

2. Seasoned investment professionals averaging 20+ years of experience with a proven strategy track record, well-resourced to exploit market inefficiencies in the SMID-cap universe.

3. Focus on companies with consistent free cash flow generation, above-average profitability, conservative balance sheets and differentiated business models with durable barriers to entry.

4. High-quality investment philosophy seeks to mitigate the risks inherent in the SMID-cap asset class, providing less volatility and potential downside mitigation.

Several of these points resonate with me. First, it is correct that many SMID-cap stocks are poor quality and overly risky, and avoiding them is a simple way to improve your chances of long-term success. Second, SMID-cap stocks don't receive nearly the same level of analyst coverage as large-caps, a fact summarized nicely below from NBSM's product brief.

NBSM's 48-stock portfolio currently includes stocks with market caps between $3.14 billion and $49.80 billion, with an average market cap of $12.28 billion. These figures indicate that analyst coverage is moderate at around 14, so the "low coverage" argument is perhaps overblown. Instead, the pitch is that the firm's analysts are better at uncovering mispricings than the consensus view on Wall Street. While this is certainly possible, the fund is still new, and we can only rely on proxy investment products and current fundamental analysis to decide if NBSM is worth its high cost.

Performance Summary

Since its inception on March 20, 2024, NBSM has delivered a 4.40% total return compared to 6.06% for the Vanguard Extended Market ETF (VXF), a popular fund that includes U.S. stocks not included in the S&P 500 Index.

The top performer in this peer group was the First Trust SMID Capital Strength ETF (FSCS), which gained 9.04% over this period and was selected for its low volatility emphasis that NBSM also prioritizes. In addition, I will assess the VanEck Morningstar SMID Moat ETF (SMOT), a relatively new offering, and the Invesco S&P MidCap Quality ETF (XMHQ), a top performer since its strategy change on June 21, 2019.

However, let's first consider the long-term returns of the Neuberger Berman Small Cap Growth Fund (NBSMX) and the Neuberger Berman Mid Cap Growth Fund (NBMLX), two open-ended no-load institutional mutual funds with 0.91% and 0.70% expense ratios. While they follow different strategies than NBSM, each has demonstrated success against low-cost benchmarks like the iShares S&P Small-Cap 600 Growth ETF (IJT) and the iShares S&P Mid-Cap 400 Growth ETF (IJK) over the last five- and ten-years.

NBSMX and NBMLX have had terrible returns since inception, gaining only 5.50% and 5.93% per year since August 2000. I'm not sure of the circumstances, but they each had substantial multi-year drawdowns in their early days. Since then, the team seems to have adopted a much more quality-focused approach, so I believe these ultra-long-term statistics are irrelevant.

NBSM Analysis

Sector Allocations and Top Ten Holdings

The following table highlights the sector allocation differences between NBSM, FSCS, SMOT, XMHQ, and VXF. At first glance, NBSM looks highly concentrated, with over 53% of its assets allocated to Industrials and Technology. Still, FSCS and XMHQ have concentration problems, too, but high allocations to Industrials is the common theme. Due to their strategies, it seems like that's where the highest-quality small/mid-cap stocks are found.

There are some common holdings, but not as much as I expected. For example, NBSM has only 8.08%, 1.28%, 12.20%, and 4.34% overlap with FSCS, SMOT, XMHQ, and VXF. This opens the door to owning more than one, which could be a good strategy if neither of these ETFs stands out.

NBSM's top ten holdings are listed below, which total 31.57% of the portfolio. ITT Inc. (ITT) and Tetra Tech (TTEK) are also included in FSCS, but in allocations of just 1.03% and 1.14%, respectively. FSCS is more diversified, as it roughly equal-weights its 100 holdings, such that only 12.11% of its top ten holdings comprise the fund.

NBSM Fundamental Analysis

The following table highlights selected fundamental metrics for NBSM's top 25 holdings, which total 64.86% of the portfolio. In this peer group, it's the least diversified and the least diversified by sub-industry, where its top 25 covers 90.81% of the fund.

Here are three additional observations to consider:

1. Except for SMOT, these ETFs have very similar weighted average market caps, so they're reasonable comparators. I first noticed that NBSM and FSCS have much lower five-year betas based on current holdings, which rank #7/49 and #1/49 among all mid-cap blend ETFs. Among all small/mid-cap funds, these betas rank #8/200, and #22/200, so risk control is the main objective.

2. NBSM has solid consensus one-year estimated sales and earnings per share growth rates of 9.76% and 9.05%, but FSCS, XMHQ, and VXF each have stronger earnings growth rates, so NBSM's growth advantage is questionable. Importantly, NBSM's 25.52x forward P/E and 18.51x trailing price-cash flow ratio are huge red flags. As you can see, several top holdings trade above 30x forward earnings, which is not typical for a mid-cap blend fund. ETFs with similar valuation ratios include the Vanguard Mid-Cap Growth ETF (VOT) and the iShares Russell Mid-Cap Growth ETF (IWP), but these offer estimated earnings growth rates of 16.55% and 19.33%, respectively.

3. NBSM has done a good job selecting high-quality stocks. Using Seeking Alpha Factor Grades, I calculated a 7.46/10 profit score, supported by the following metrics:

If your goal is to improve the quality of low-cost extended market funds like VXF, NBSM works. However, FSCS has better quality metrics in many cases, including a weighted average ROE that's 6.42% higher (24.85% vs. 18.43%). Given that NBSM's strengths are low volatility and high quality, it's difficult to justify an investment, especially considering FSCS trades at an eight-point discount on forward earnings while offering similar growth, quality, and much better diversification.

Investment Recommendation

NBSM's 0.74% net expense ratio may be a dealbreaker for some readers, but my biggest issue is the 25.52x forward P/E ratio. Typically, these valuations are reserved for ETFs with high expected sales and earnings growth rates, but that is not the case here, as NBSM's growth rates are only average for the size segment. While I expect it to perform relatively well in a market downturn due to its low beta and high-quality features, FSCS is a more effective option that trades at a 31% forward P/E discount, and I believe it's prudent to hedge your bets with this cheaper, better-diversified ETF. As a result, I have assigned NBSM a "sell" rating, and I look forward to answering your questions in the comments below. Thank you for reading.

data.title: H&R Block: This Boring Buyback King Still Has Room To Run
data.description:
Despite shares moving up almost 40% in the last year and almost 160% in the last five years, boring tax preparation company H&R Block (NYSE:HRB) is interesting today because of its attractive share repurchase program, reasonable valuation, and growth potential from multiple sources.

Introduction

H&R Block was founded in 1955 by Henry and Richard Bloch.

The two brothers ran a successful accounting business whose primary focus was bookkeeping. It offered tax preparation as a courtesy to customers and friends, but the brothers decided they would stop the practice because it wasn't a significant source of revenue for the business. John White, a customer, told the brothers that he thought with just a little bit of advertising, a potential tax business would take off.

The Blochs reluctantly agreed to run two ads in The Kansas City Star, and the results were tremendous. The idea immediately took off, buoyed by the IRS's decision to stop offering free tax preparation services for Kansas City. The brothers then expanded the concept to other cities -- New York City was next -- where the IRS was scheduled to discontinue its tax preparation services.

The company steadily expanded throughout the United States over the next 40 years, including expanding operations into Canada and Australia. It has prepared 935M tax returns worldwide since its founding, including more than 20M in 2023 alone. The company has also expanded into other financial service areas, including Block Advisors, which offers small business tax advice, and Wave, digital accounting software. It also owns Spruce, a mobile banking platform with nearly $1B in consumer deposits and almost 500,000 customers.

A sizable minority of taxpayers choose to prepare their own tax returns, using either the IRS's free online software or using one of the various tax preparation software services on the market. Intuit's TurboTax is the dominant leader here. But despite the many advantages offered by the various pieces of software, a slim majority of Americans still choose to get a professional to help them with their taxes.

This phenomenon has persisted for years now, even amid growth of tax preparation software.

The United States tax code is complex, and it often changes from year to year. The average taxpayer looks at what is likely their most complicated financial transaction of the year and will often enlist the help of a trusted professional. The professional help allows them to feel a little better about the transaction, even if it is a higher cost. H&R Block often gets that business because customers are attracted to the strength of the brand and the convenience of having a location nearby. There are more than 8,800 locations in the United States alone, with an additional 952 in Canada and 397 in Australia.

Income tax returns are also an incredibly stable business, growing inline with population growth. The vast majority of taxpayers file a return each year, motivated mostly by their tax refund. Approximately 75% of Americans receive a tax refund each year, with the average refund nearing $3,000.

Both DIY tax preparation software and assisted tax return preparation are competitive businesses. But H&R's competition is largely local in nature. It competes with accounting and bookkeeping firms that might have a solid local foundation, but can't boast the same kind of brand recognition or coast-to-coast presence H&R Block has. In addition, customers are somewhat sticky, since there are certain advantages to going to the same tax preparer as last year. These both contribute to H&R Block's moat.

Growth outlook

Many investors take a quick glance at H&R Block, declare it a no-growth dinosaur, and immediately move onto other investments. But the company has done a satisfactory job growing the top line, as well as using a robust share repurchase program to really grow the bottom line. That's the exciting part here.

Let's first focus on H&R Block's revenue growth, which is hardly spectacular. According to the company's income statement, it generated a hair over $3B in revenue in 2016. A decade later, in generated just over $3.6B in revenue. That only translates into revenue growth of 20% over a decade -- or a little less than 2% per year.

There aren't many investors that will get excited about that.

However, revenue growth has been better lately, with the top-line increasing from $3.47B in fiscal 2023 to $3.61B in fiscal 2024. That's 4% growth over the last year. Analysts are also relatively bullish on the revenue front, with consensus estimates projecting revenue will increase to $3.72B in fiscal 2025 and a further increase to $3.82B in fiscal 2026. That works out to anticipated revenue growth of just over 3% per year, a marked improvement from the 2015-24 period.

So what will drive this revenue growth? The company projects long-term revenue growth targets of 3-6% per year, driven by a combination of population growth, price increases, franchise acquisitions, and upside from both Wave and Block Horizons -- including further expansion of Spruce, the mobile banking platform.

The company has provided its 2025 outlook that projects a revenue increase of approximately 3-4%, and adjusted earnings that are between 16% and 21% higher than 2024's adjusted earnings of $4.41 per share. These results are expected to benefit from a one-time tax benefit of approximately $0.50 per share.

While long-term revenue growth has hardly been spectacular, where H&R Block really shines is delivering earnings growth. It does this by using a combination of cost controls and consistently generating strong cash flows, but mostly via the company's share repurchase program.

To fully understand just how successful H&R Block has been at repurchasing shares, let us take a closer look at earnings versus earnings per share. In fiscal 2015, the company earned $383M in earnings. That works out to $1.54 per share based on 249M shares outstanding.

Fast-forward to 2024. H&R Block increased earnings nicely, boosting the bottom line to $596M. That boost, combined with a substantial decrease in shares outstanding, increased earnings on a per share basis to $4.14 per share.

In short, earnings increased by approximately 56%, while earnings per share increased by 169%. This was driven by a 43% decrease in shares outstanding from the end of fiscal 2015 to fiscal 2024.

The company has no plans to stop the share buyback, either. In fiscal 2024, it repurchased another 8M shares, retiring another 5.5% of shares outstanding. It spent $350M on those purchases. It also filed for authorization to spend an additional $1.5B on more share buybacks, with CFO Tony Bowen hinting the company is likely to spend approximately $350M again on buybacks in 2025.

H&R Block does have some interesting revenue growth potential, and this analyst thinks the company's target of 3-6% revenue growth is reasonable. But the most exciting growth feature here is the share buyback program, boosting earnings on a per share basis.

The company has also amassed a decent record of dividend growth. With its latest announcement to increase dividends by 17%, H&R Block now has increased its dividends per share by nearly 100% since 2015. The payout has increased from $0.20 per share in 2015 to $0.375 per share today. That works out to a 2.5% dividend yield today.

Valuation

Despite shares performing exceptionally well as of late, H&R Block is hardly an expensive stock.

Over the last year, the company's shares increased by nearly 40%.

Results were even better over the last five years, with shares increasing by nearly 160%. I'll note that neither of these two charts include dividends, which have further boosted returns.

Despite these excellent returns, this analyst believes H&R Block shares are still reasonably valued.

Consensus analyst estimates expect the company to earn $5.26 per share in 2025, earnings buoyed by the one-time tax benefit. The bottom line is then expected to decrease slightly in 2026, settling in at $5.12 per share.

Both those numbers put the stock at under 12x forward earnings expectations. That's a very reasonable number.

The company also has an excellent recent history of beating analyst expectation. It has beaten analyst earnings expectations in 11 of its last 12 quarters, and it has beaten analyst revenue expectations in 10 of its last 12 quarters. If this trend continues, earnings could surprise on the upside, which would further help today's valuation.

This is also a significant discount to the valuation of the market itself, with the S&P 500 trading at approximately 23x forward earnings.

Risks

This analyst sees one major risk that could impact H&R Block, and that's further innovation from the leader in DIY tax preparation software.

Pundits have expected TurboTax to take a big bite out of H&R Block's business for years, and it just hasn't materialized. Customers want to know they have a professional in their corner in case something goes wrong, plus they enjoy being able to discuss their return with someone.

TurboTax has tried to grow its full-service offering, but it's largely online based at this point. It doesn't offer the same kind of experience as going down to the local H&R Block branch and talking to a real-life person.

Still, there is the risk that Intuit significantly expands this service, which would erode H&R Block's moat.

The bottom line

H&R Block is the kind of boring stock this analyst likes. It has an obvious moat, reasonable growth expectations, and it gushes cash flow. It has generated solid profits for decades now.

But the share buyback program is the real star here. It has allowed a mature business to grow earnings on a per share basis pretty substantially over the last decade. Management has no plans to stop, either.

Combine that with today's reasonable valuation and a nice history of dividend growth, and H&R Block checks off a lot of boxes for readers who enjoy boring, blue-chip dividend stocks.

data.title: LPL Financial: Near-Term Overhang Is A Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Over the years, leading broker-dealer LPL Financial (NASDAQ:LPLA) has built up a best-in-class platform spanning front, middle, and back-office solutions for financial advisors. The results speak for themselves - ~23.5k advisors and 1.5 trillion assets onboarded (as of Q2 2024), as well as a best-in-class rate of earnings compounding through the cycles.

A lot of LPL's success has occurred under the leadership of (former) President and CEO Dan Arnold, so his abrupt departure after almost seven years in charge is a negative surprise. There are silver linings, though, as I dive into later in the article, with interim CEO and former Chief Growth Officer Steinmeier standing out as a very worthy successor. Other sources of volatility, including interest rate shifts, seem manageable as well. In the meantime, LPL's historically discounted low-teens earnings multiple (vs. high-teens % historical EPS growth) offers investors a compelling entry point.

Silver Linings to the CEO Departure…

LPL started this month with a big negative surprise, announcing that long-tenured President and CEO Dan Arnold, on the "recommendation of a special committee of Directors in the course of an investigation by an outside law firm", had been terminated for cause due code of conduct violations (see full 8-K filing here). In his place, Rich Steinmeier, LPL's (also long-tenured) Chief Growth Officer, will take on the interim CEO role.

Sudden CEO reshuffles are never ideal, but timing-wise, it's worth noting that LPL is also in the midst of several major platform integrations. Chief among them is the acquisition and subsequent onboarding of wealth management solutions provider Atria (closed Oct 1st, 2024) and its advisor base through mid-2025 (80% retention targeted). There's also the migration of Prudential's wealth business, which, per CEO Arnold on the Q2 call, was on track for a year-end completion.

Still, there are silver linings here. For one, the fallout from this incident(s) appears to be contained to Arnold. Secondly, that LPL's bench is deep and well-proven. Starting with interim CEO (and most likely successor) Steinmeier, who has been spearheading business strategy and onboarding new financial advisors and institutions. As for the finance side of things, there's current CFO Matt Audette, who has worked hand-in-hand with Arnold and Steinmeier in driving LPL's growth through the last seven years. In sum, all signs point to business as usual for the LPL flywheel.

…while Monthly Data Reveals a Favorable Setup into Earnings

In the meantime, the company's monthly data releases add valuable insight to how the upcoming Q3 numbers are shaping up. At first glance, the latest activity report through August shows some relatively disappointing metrics, starting with a decline in client cash balances relative to last month and a seemingly below-par net new asset growth rate.

There are, however, a few caveats here. Starting with the cash movement, it's worth noting that the headline month-on-month comparison incorporates an unfavorable base effect from July, during which stronger cash inflows led to a higher-than-usual month-end cash balance. Also, that LPL is working through some churn in its RIA (or "registered investment advisor") base after starting a "strategic land grab" earlier this year in an effort to break up some of the larger RIA aggregators under its banner (specifically targeting "offices of supervisory jurisdiction" or OSJs, larger practices that help with the oversight of smaller practices).

Similarly, headline monthly asset growth numbers don't tell the full story. While LPL's +5.2% "organic" net new asset growth rate (+7.7% Advisory and +1.9% Brokerage) is below historical levels, the figure includes the asset outflow from offboarding two major OSJs worth ~$20bn of assets. Excluding the ~$5bn or so of outflows so far, underlying organic asset growth is really running ahead of guidance at over 8% (advisory in the low teens %).

From here, capital appreciation from a bullish US market should further accelerate underlying asset growth through September. Hence, all signs point to a beat-and-raise quarter from LPL come Q3 results (due at month-end).

…and Rate Concerns Remain Well-Contained

The other key LPL debate is around its ability to monetize customer cash balances. More specifically, with regard to its 'cash sweep', or the yield earned when 'sweeping' excess client cash from brokerage to investment accounts. On the one hand, you have declining interest rates, which will almost certainly reverse a lot of the earnings tailwind LPL previously enjoyed when rates were rising.

Then you've also got pressure from a string of civil class action suits aimed at the cash sweep practices of LPL and its peers. The silver lining is that so far, regulatory scrutiny has been focused at the wirehouse level rather than directly at LPL. And more recently, LPL, along with brokerage peers like Schwab (SCHW) have been quick to lower cash sweep rates in response to the Fed's 50bps rate cut. So quick, in fact, that it may well be indicative of a well-contained regulatory fallout from here.

Over the mid to longer run, what matters most, in my view, is that LPL has levers to contain a worst-case scenario for its 'sweep' rates. Take, for instance, management pre-empting the worst of the rate cut impact by investing a large chunk of LPL's cash deposits in fixed rate contracts (~70% of the "insured cash account" portfolio as of Q2 2024).

Lower rates (minus a recession) tend to coincide with bull markets as well, so LPL can also lean on higher fee income and commissions, or even additional fees, to offset some of the interest rate impact. Alternatively, there's the option to cut back on expenses to protect margins, though this would also come at the expense of reinvestments (and, by extension, future growth); hence, a less attractive lever, in my view.

Final Note on Risks & Summing Up

With or without Dan Arnold, the LPL flywheel is spinning. And with the rest of the team still intact, I don't see the recent management reshuffle derailing this growth story. Instead, I'd keep a closer eye on the other risks at play, including how LPL manages market movements, interest rates, and regulatory pressures heading into earnings later this month. Either way, this is mostly still the same senior management team that successfully navigated prior cycles, so the stock is in great hands. Having seen its P/E dip into the low-teens in recent months, I would use the near-term LPL overhang as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Steady Yields, Shaky Growth: NVE Corporation's Spin On The Semi Market
data.description:
Thesis

NVE Corporation (NASDAQ:NVEC), a spintronics pioneer that’s been around since 1989, is about to unveil its Q2 2025 earnings tomorrow, October 16, 2024. Now, on paper, everything looks good—solid financials, impressive margins, and dividends that investors can count on like clockwork. However, the company’s growth story seems a bit murkier: recent revenue has been on a slight downward drift, and the ever-volatile semiconductor market isn’t exactly making things easy, which makes me think investors should seriously consider balancing NVE’s long-term potential with its innovative tech against the current market challenges.

Spintronics Technology Overview

NVE Corporation, under the helm of CEO Dr. Daniel Baker, is a modestly sized outfit, employing around 54 people, and has been quietly doing its thing since 1989 from the unlikely tech hub of Eden Prairie, Minnesota. The company’s specialty is Spintronics. Now, for those not entirely up to speed on the finer points of nanotechnology, spintronics deals with the rather clever idea of using the spin of electrons to process and transmit information. It’s a fascinating field, if you’re into that sort of thing, and NVE has been putting it to practical use by developing all sorts of gadgets, like sensors and couplers, which find their way into everything from factory automation systems to industrial networks, and even medical devices.

Core Spintronics Technologies

NVE’s spintronic devices rely on two key technologies: Giant Magnetoresistance (GMR) and Tunneling Magnetoresistance (TMR). These help the products detect magnetic fields and measure resistance changes with high accuracy. This is huge for saving power and ensuring reliable data. You’ll find these devices in industries like robotics and autonomous systems, where precise data matters most.

Applications of Spintronics

NVE’s new-generation spintronics solution has attracted an astonishing number of customers in fields such as industrial automation, medical technology, and the military. Businesses such as Cypress and Freescale have followed suit, adopting NVE’s ingenious spintronics to create what they’ve dubbed Magnetoresistive Random-Access Memory, or MRAM for short. This new memory is faster, stronger, and more power-efficient than other types of memory, so it's a potential paradigm shifter for many use cases.

Advantages in Mobile and Defense

Spintronics revolutionizes all of our hyperefficient, high-performance technology, from smartphones to artificial intelligence chips and space platforms. MRAM, for instance, reduces CPU load, resulting in less heat and lighter designs – great news for mobile phones demanding high performance with long battery lives. But it is equally great in sectors that require high-performance, energy-efficient electronics, particularly in environments such as space or military. These devices operate under harsh conditions of temperature, magnetic fields, and radiation, making them an excellent candidate for defense and aerospace.

Long-Term Spintronics Potential

Spintronics could eventually rival semiconductors of today. It requires less power, scales, and will work with current chip-design techniques, which means it could be replacing or coexisting with legacy technologies in the future. Overall, spintronics might lead to breakthroughs, as semiconductor memory did decades ago. This will be hugely influential for quantum computing, the Internet of Things, and AI tech going forward.

Performance Snapshot Since FY2016

A quick personal flashback: during my high-frequency trading days with RBC from 2004 to 2011, NVEC was a common trade for us due to its volatility. This brings me to its more recent performance. I’ve captured its performance from FY2016 to provide a snapshot of its potential. While extending this to a 20-year time frame shows even stronger stats, I thought it would be more helpful to meet investors and potential investors halfway to give a clearer general picture.

Over the past nine years, NVE Corporation has had some good points but also some weaknesses. When it comes to overall returns, it hasn’t done as well as the broader market, like the SPY index. NVEC’s yearly return, not counting dividends, is just 1.46%, which is much lower than SPY’s 11.45%. The difference gets even bigger when you include growth and dividends. NVEC’s growth rate is 5.70%, while SPY’s is 12.44%. So, while NVEC provides steady returns, it doesn’t grow as much as you’d expect compared to the broader market.

NVEC has managed to carve out a niche by doing one thing very well: paying dividends. For the last nine years, they’ve dished out a steady $4.00 per share, like clockwork with the yield on cost—a respectable 5.78%—is enough to get the attention of cautious investors who’d rather sit back and watch their money grow than take any wild risks.

But I've come across some comments from investors who think NVEC is being too generous with the dividends and that the money could be better spent chasing growth. But in truth, NVEC’s solid average dividend growth rate of 10.46% speaks to its healthy cash flow, with a compound growth rate of 7.65% that even manages to beat out the SPY’s 6.28%.

Q2 2025 Earnings Preview

As of now, NVEC is set to release its Q2 2025 earnings tomorrow (10/16/2024) after the market closes, so let's take a quick look at how they're doing and what we might expect to learn. Despite a 23% drop in total revenue during Q1, there was a solid bright spot—contract research and development revenue jumped 28%. These (contract) projects often tap into NVE’s expertise in spintronics and nanotechnology, helping clients solve engineering problems or develop cutting-edge tech. So instead of selling products, the revenue here comes from offering high-end research services. Building on this positive development, another key win from Q1 was the significant boost in gross margin, climbing to 86% from 77% last year, thanks to a more profitable product mix and a stronger emphasis on direct sales.

Semiconductor Industry Recovery

Another heartening sign for NVE is the much-anticipated recovery of the semiconductor industry, which, it has to be said, hasn’t been particularly kind to their product sales of late. It’s expected to bounce back through the end of 2024, with management already waving the flag for strong growth in 2025. This year, the semiconductor market is expected to expand by a rather healthy 16-20%, reaching a tidy sum of around $611 billion by year’s end. Some forecasts suggest they’ll surge 76%, largely thanks to the insatiable appetites of AI and high-performance computing.

As for geography, the Americas and Asia-Pacific regions are set to enjoy the lion’s share of the growth, with double-digit gains. Europe and Japan, on the other hand, will probably have to content themselves with more modest increases.

Looking ahead to 2025, the recovery is expected to continue with an additional 12-13% growth, bringing the market up to nearly $687 billion. This sustained surge will largely be driven by the Memory and Logic segments—both riding high on the back of AI integration.

Future Growth and Investments

In the meantime, NVE is planning for growth, committing $4-5 million to capital investment over the next two years (arguably small given the dividend ratio and considering the time frame, in my opinion) to increase its manufacturing capacity. It’s about wafer-level chip-scale packaging, which could provide new revenue streams and margins. Customer interest in these products is already being registered by management, which makes a solid proposition for future sales.

In addition to manufacturing, NVE is scaling its sales and marketing, hiring new employees, and attending key trade shows in the industry. Plus, with a 26% increase in R&D investment and the introduction of new evaluation boards, NVE is already aggressively focusing on innovation and preparing for further product releases, which should contribute to long-term sales.

Finally, NVE’s growth outlook gets another boost from its expanding defense-related product line. The company expects this sector to stay strong, especially with its Physical Unclonable Functions (PUFs), which are in high demand as more focus shifts to autonomous defense systems and anti-tamper tech. This ongoing demand suggests stable, if not increasing, revenue in this area.

NVEC's Valuation

To assess NVEC's valuation, I substituted the missing Adjusted (Operating) Earnings Growth Rate on Fast Tracks by calculating the compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of the company's Operating Income from 2015 to 2024, which resulted in approximately -1.08%.

In place of a blended P/E ratio, I used NVEC's current P/E (TTM) of 22.90. That’s higher than its fair value of 15.00x but lower than its usual 26.39x and immediately suggests some undervaluation, though it also shows investors expect future growth. The issue? Having a high P/E ratio while growth is shrinking? That’s a warning sign.

On the bright side is the dividend yield I mentioned earlier at 5.03%, which is solid for income-seekers. NVEC has very low debt, with a debt-to-capital ratio of 0.20%, giving it maximum flexibility in payouts.

NVEC’s market cap is $384.08 million, making it a smaller player with only 54 employees in a highly competitive semiconductor industry. Being small has its perks, like agility in niche markets, but NVEC may not have the muscle to overcome broader growth challenges. And since we’re on the subject of small, although it may or may not matter, its website is rocking that late 90s, early 2000s vibe and is long overdue for a modern upgrade if it wants to match that "disruptive" image.

Anyways, aesthetic opinion aside, with a total enterprise value (TEV) of $363.12 million, its valuation leans on equity, not debt, and that’s good for its balance sheet. But a strong balance sheet alone won’t drive long-term growth.

Risks and Market Challenges

That said, several bearish factors are cooling the optimism around NVE's performance. It's worth repeating that total revenue dropped 23%, with product sales down 24%, highlighting the challenges from the semiconductor slump and high inventory levels. Despite strong margins, total expenses went up 3%. And those R&D costs that surged 26%, and selling, general, and administrative expenses that climbed 14%—they could come back and squeeze future profits.

Furthermore, net income dropped 7%, showing the hit from lower revenues despite a higher gross margin and a favorable tax rate. The slump in the semiconductor industry, along with high distributor inventory levels, continues to be a major drag on NVE's core business. Although the company plans to invest $4-5 million to expand manufacturing, there’s a risk this move won’t lead to immediate revenue growth since customer orders for the new wafer-level chip-scale products haven't come in yet.

Final Takeaway

I'd call this stock a solid 'Hold.' Now, don't get me wrong—NVE Corporation is doing fine financially. They’ve got solid margins, a reliable dividend, and nothing scary popping up on the balance sheet. That said, their growth outlook is pretty cloudy right now, revenue’s dipped, and the semiconductor market isn’t exactly running full steam. Yes, their spintronics tech is cool and has big potential down the road, but with no clear sign of strong customer demand just yet, jumping in headfirst doesn’t seem like the best move right now.

data.title: General Electric: The Razor Blade Business Model
data.description:
Introduction

After a long and winding road, General Electric (NYSE:GE) was restructured and split into three businesses, each with strengths and weaknesses. I recently covered GE Vernova (GEV) and competitor (OTCPK:RYCEY) Rolls-Royce and now dive into the aerospace division that kept the GE ticker symbol. I found a world-class business with a rightfully premium valuation driven by a business model that should deliver high returns for 20 years on each engine sold.

Service: The razor blade business model

GE Aerospace owns 50% of the commercial jet engine market, with more than 44k units in service, which require 20 years of maintenance and produce around 3.5x the price of a new engine. A similar situation exists in the defense engine market, with 26k engines in service. Thus, every new engine sold, for an estimated US$10m -US$30m for narrow/wide body airplanes, means that GE can perpetuate service revenue of approximately US$3.5m per engine/year for 20 years. About 70% of GE's revenue comes from services, which should gradually increase as new engine growth outpaces airplane decommissioning rates over the next ten years.

Another way to illustrate (Fig 1) the value of the service revenue is to look at the backlog; GE reports the approximate value of forward service obligations, which have steadily increased and tower over new engine orders. This is a key reason for the stock's high valuation; there is a high degree of earnings visibility.

Below (Fig 4), I estimate the growth and EBIT margin for the commercial and defense business using company guidance, which has been increasing. I estimate that commercial services account for 72% of EBIT and can grow at around 14% a year. The poorest margin business is the defense engines. However, in defense, the R&D is generally paid for by the military, which means far less invested capital is required, and thus, a low margin does not mean a low return.

The commercial engine segment may see a deceleration in deliveries due to the normalization of demand in the long term. In contrast, in the short term, the continued supply chain issues at Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) and Boeing (BA) may delay growth. During the pandemic, many airlines mothballed older airplanes, and when demand returned, they scrambled for capacity and new, more efficient, and cost-effective airplanes, resulting in a demand spike. The industry is still working through this, and Boeing’s problems add to airline capacity constraints, but the situation has provided the engine and other OEMs with pricing power.

Financial Estimates

Using the revenue and EBIT model data from Figure 4, I calculated GE earnings and cash flow. The company should be able to grow consolidated revenue by 10%. With margin gains on equipment pricing power, it may grow cash earnings (net income + depreciation) by over 20% in the 2025-26 period. I used capex, dividend, and buyback guidance to calculate cash flow, which indicates the company may need to add some debt.

Overall, the estimates are modestly lower than the current consensus, with some room for upgrades if GE grows equipment orders above the 7% range in 2025. Note that the data from 2021-2023 is built by subtracting GEV from GE and are estimates post-company split.

Valuation

The consensus has a US$206 year-end 2025 price target for GE, which backs into an implied P/cash earnings multiple of 34x or a relative PEG ratio of 1.7x. This is above the market but in line with the aerospace sector and indicative of GE's premium valuation based on its high degree of long-term earnings visibility and service backlog. Using this same 1.7x PEG, I calculated a US$246 price target for YE26 or 29% upside potential.

Peer Comps

I gathered consensus data on aircraft makers, OEMs, and defense contractors to compare with GE's growth and valuation metrics. Overall, the sector is highly valued by the market and viewed as resilient with good long-term earnings visibility. The consensus does not ascribe much upside to most stocks, except for BA and Airbus, which have been more challenged in meeting production guidance. GE does not materially stand out vs. selected peers.

Risk

The primary risk of holding the share is that the market turns negative on valuation and downgrades the stock. The 1.7x PEG or 34x P/Cash Earnings is above the S&P 500 (SPX), which means if GE does not meet or beat quarterly expectations and raises guidance, the market may deem it expensive. On the operating side, the main risk is if BA does not eventually turn around and sees a permanent reduction in production capacity. GE can also suffer market share erosion that would dent long-term service growth.

Conclusion

I rate GE a hold. The company's market dominance in jet engines should allow it to track the airline's capacity growth. At the same time, the longevity of said engines and required maintenance provide GE with a very long runway of revenue, earnings, and cash flow growth and visibility. This is tempered by a premium valuation that requires meeting or beating quarterly results for modest upside potential. The risk-reward symmetry does not seem compelling.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: 2 High-Yielding Dividend Picks To Fund Your Retirement
data.description:
I have been quite actively covering high quality BDCs, equity REITs and MLPs that offer decent dividend yields with all of the necessary financial properties in place to mitigate the risk of distribution cut.

The main focus point with these kinds of assets is to facilitate a slow and steady income compounding process that would, ultimately, once the retirement age kicks in, develop into a durable income portfolio, which could, in turn, fund a large portion of living expenses.

To make this happen, there has to be an emphasis not only on yield but also on above-inflation level growth that is underpinned by truly robust financials.

However, some investors might already from the start prefer high yield and safety without the income growth component due to, for example, a need to immediately spend the current income streams or just magnify the capital for making periodic reinvestments. Similarly, there could also be a merit of including such securities to enhance the overall portfolio yield and stimulate diversification, while still keeping in place the objective of durable and attractive income growth.

With this in mind, let me now present two high-yielding investment picks, which, in my opinion, could be easily included in retirement income seeking portfolios.

Pick #1 - Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund

Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund (JPC) is an actively managed closed end fund, providing a monthly yield of 9.8%. It has a clear strategy to deploy capital into mostly preferred securities and to a smaller degree in other below senior debt or bond fixed income instruments such as hybrid bonds and convertibles.

Already here there is a benefit of accessing current income streams, which are stipulated in financing agreements and are more protected than stocks. Granted, we are not talking about senior or investment grade bonds, which could be considered an even safer play, but still, due to massive exposure to preferred shares, JPC is less volatile from an income perspective than similar funds with pure play equity investments.

Speaking of the risks, they are further mitigated through the following two factors:

  • JPC is diversified across ~ 345 securities, where the Top 5 accounts only for 7.5% of the total exposure.

  • This is very critical to appreciate - about 80% of the total exposure is placed in investment grade or upper investment grade investments, which materially de-risk the overall position. The current exposure to IG securities is clearly above the Fund's mandate to hold at least 50% of such assets.

Finally, one could theoretically make a case against JPC for being included in retirement portfolios by pinpointing to the presence of external leverage, which, as of now, explains roughly 37% of the Fund's asset base.

However, when it comes to external leverage, usually, the key risks lie within the cost of debt levels, when the financing has been assumed at below market level interest rates. It means that once such borrowings get refinanced at higher interest rates, there is automatically a notable pressure put on the existing distributions (with a high chance of leading to a distribution cut).

The good news in JPC's case is that the current cost of debt stands at ~ 6.3%, which is largely reflective of the prevailing market level financing rates. So, going forward, the chances of JPC announcing a reduction in distributions due to ballooning debt service costs are extremely limited, especially considering the current normalization process in base rate levels.

All in all, JPC is a safe choice for investors, who want to maximize the yield level already from the start, while not putting a heavy emphasize on income growth, which the Fund cannot deliver in a meaningful fashion due to inherent limitations that come from preferred security exposures (i.e., fixed income like dynamics).

Pick #2 - Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF

The Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD) is an even higher yielding monthly pick, which currently offers a dividend of 11.35%. At its core, QYLD is a pure play covered call ETF, which sells options against the Nasdaq-100 index in order to enhance the yield potential.

Before I explain in a more detail why QYLD could be deemed as an enticing pick to boost current income streams now, let's first understand how the covered call mechanics work:

  1. QYLD goes long the Nasdaq 100 index, which initially replicates the full set of risks and returns that would come from a simple investment in the index.
  2. Then QYLD sells calls against its long exposure to Nasdaq 100. This way, QYLD collects premiums that are associated with these sold call options.
  3. The calls are sold out of the money, which means that not the entire upside is limited. Namely, there is usually some value that QYLD can capture from a rising index.
  4. If the index surpasses the option strike value that was sold by QYLD, any gains above that point are lost (i.e., not captured by QYLD unitholders).
  5. If the index declines, QYLD keeps the option premiums, which help offset all or part of the registered downside at the index level.

Another important element here to recognize is that the key driver for option premiums (i.e., amounts QYLD receives from selling covered calls) is the level of VIX or implied market volatility. Namely, the higher uncertainty in the markets, the more money QYLD received, which it can then distribute in higher dividends.

The worst case what could happen is if the markets are calm and the Nasdaq-100 slowly but surely declines in value.

Yet, in my humble opinion, this is a very unlikely scenario. It is hard to imagine how the large cap names such as Tesla, Inc. (TSLA), Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) or Apple (AAPL) could lose their market cap levels without the overall Nasdaq-100 index not experiencing elevated volatility levels.

In other words, we could think of QYLD as a quasi fixed income instrument, which has a range bound payoff profile with the underlying yields subject to volatility, where even in the worst-case scenario, the dividend would remain fairly attractive.

This is what we can also see in the 5-year dividend distribution chart below. The lowest dividend yield in this time period was ~9%, when the VIX was extremely depressed, and the market was trading sideways.

In fact, if we look at the historical 5-year VIX average levels, we will see rather low and stable levels throughout the entire period, in which QYLD has still managed to deliver truly enticing dividends to its shareholders.

Given the above, it would be fair to conclude that QYLD could fit nicely into high income-seeking portfolios, where the investors are agnostic to price volatility, and instead are mostly focused on capturing abnormal dividends to boost their portfolio overall yield levels.

The bottom line

While for many income investors, accessing reasonable dividend growth is necessary to maximize the compounding process, there could be a case for sacrificing growth to achieve truly abnormal current income streams.

For example, it could make sense to allocate into safe high income producing assets if the goal is to already from the start rely on portfolio income or just tactically boost the overall portfolio yield level. Accessing attractive dividends, especially as in the case of JPC and QYLD, which distribute them on a monthly basis, can even nicely complement periodic contributions to the portfolio.

Based on the financial details above around JPC and QYLD, I would feel comfortable including both of these names into a retirement income portfolio in order to enhance the monthly income levels.
